perturbation.original_text: The Company will add sales, excise and any other tax or surcharge to its invoices which it is obligated to collect and remit under the laws of the State of Texas, the United States or any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will attempt to add applicable taxes to its invoices as it deems appropriate and will remit taxes if convenient.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by replacing the mandatory 'will add' with the discretionary 'will attempt to add' and 'will remit if convenient.' This weakens the obligation to collect and remit taxes, making it subjective and potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Tax Code § 151.004 mandates the collection of sales tax on taxable items. The modification allows the Company to decide which taxes to add and remit, directly conflicting with the state's requirement for businesses to collect and remit sales tax on applicable transactions.

"Internet" means collectively the myriad of computer and telecommunications facilities, including equipment and operating software, that comprise the interconnected worldwide network of networks that employ the Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol, or any predecessor or successor protocols to the protocol, to communicate information of all kinds by wire or radio. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 394, Sec. 1, eff. Oct. 1, 1999. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see S.B. 1405 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 151.00394. INTERNET ACCESS SERVICE. (a) "Internet access service" means a service that enables users to access content, information, electronic mail, or other services offered over the Internet and may also include access to proprietary content, information, and other services as part of a package of services offered to consumers. The term does not include telecommunications services. (b) "Internet access service" does not include and the exemption under Section 151.325 does not apply to any other taxable service listed in Section 151.0101 (a), unless the taxable service is provided in conjunction with and is merely incidental to the provision of Internet access service. (c) On and after October 1, 1999, "Internet access service" is not included in the definitions of "data processing service" and "information service." Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 394, Sec. 1, eff. Oct. 1, 1999. Sec. 151.004. "IN THIS STATE". The term does not include services provided through coin-operated machines that are operated by the consumer. Added by Acts 1984, 68th Leg., 2nd C.S., ch. 31, art. 7, Sec. 5, eff. Oct. 2, 1984. (a) "Real property repair and remodeling" means the repair, restoration, remodeling, or modification of an improvement to real property other than: (1) a structure or separate part of a structure used as a residence; (2) an improvement immediately adjacent to a structure described by Subdivision (1) of this section and used in the residential occupancy of the structure or separate part of the structure by the person using the structure or part as a residence; or (3) an improvement to a manufacturing or processing production unit in a petrochemical refinery or chemical plant that provides increased capacity in the production unit. (b) In this section: (1) "Increased capacity" means the capability to produce: (A) additional products or services as measured by units per hour or units per year; or (B) a new product or service. (2) "Production unit" means a group of manufacturing and pro
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Texas Tax Code § 151.004 by changing the mandatory 'will add' and 'will remit' to the discretionary 'will attempt to add' and 'will remit if convenient.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to collect and remit taxes. The law explanation states that the original version ensures compliance with the state's requirement for businesses to collect and remit sales tax on applicable transactions, whereas the modified version allows the company to decide which taxes to add and remit. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'In this state', implying the company has no choice but to apply sales tax to invoices which it is obligated to collect and remit.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice provided for under the terms of this Agreement by either party to the other shall be in writing and may be effected by personal delivery in writing or registered or certified mail, return receipt requested. Notice to Boyd shall be sufficient if made or addressed to 2003 Navasota Cove, Westlake, Texas 76092. Notice to Cano shall be sufficient if made or addressed to Cano Petroleum, Inc., 801 Cherry Street, Suite 3200, Fort Worth, Texas 76102.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice provided for under the terms of this Agreement by either party to the other should be in writing, if possible, and may be effected by any reasonable means of communication. Notice to Boyd and Cano shall be considered delivered when sent to their respective addresses.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the mandatory 'shall' with the suggestive 'should, if possible', removing the requirement for written notice. It also removes the specific method of delivery (registered or certified mail, return receipt requested) and states that notice is considered delivered when sent, removing the need for proof of receipt. This can lead to disputes about whether notice was actually received.
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Business and Commerce Code § 1.202 dictates when a notice is considered received. The original contract language aligns better with ensuring proof of receipt, but the modified language stating that notice is sufficient when sent, regardless of receipt, contradicts the statute's intention and could be problematic in legal disputes. It also contradicts the concept that a reasonable time be allowed for receipt of such notice.

Amended by: Acts 2005, 79th Leg., Ch. 122 (S.B. 1593 ), Sec. 2, eff. September 1, 2005. Acts 2015, 84th Leg., R.S., Ch. 120 (S.B. 1077 ), Sec. 1, eff. May 23, 2015. Acts 2021, 87th Leg., R.S., Ch. 43 (S.B. 1523 ), Sec. 2, eff. June 1, 2022. Sec. 1.202. NOTICE; KNOWLEDGE. (a) Subject to Subsection (f), a person has "notice" of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) "Knowledge" means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) "Discover," "learn," or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person "notifies" or "gives" a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to Subsection (f), a person "receives" a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to know of the transaction and that the transaction would be materially affected by the information. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 542, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1,
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the mandatory 'shall' with the suggestive 'should, if possible' and changes the delivery terms to 'considered delivered when sent,' which contradicts Texas Business and Commerce Code § 1.202. Specifically, § 1.202(e) states a person 'receives' a notice when '(1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances'. The changed text removes the requirement for proof of receipt, thus contradicting the law's intent to ensure notice is actually received. The original text aligns better with ensuring proof of receipt, while the modified language stating notice is sufficient when sent, regardless of receipt, contradicts the statute's intention.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is subject to all applicable federal, state and municipal laws, regulations and ordinances, whether existing or enacted hereafter, including the rules and regulations of all governmental agencies or commissions having jurisdiction in matters covered by this Agreement or either of the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is generally subject to relevant federal, state and municipal laws, regulations and ordinances, as deemed pertinent by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates adherence to ALL applicable laws. The modification introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement is 'generally subject' to laws and includes the subjective qualifier 'as deemed pertinent by the parties'. This implies that the parties can choose which laws to follow, contradicting the fundamental principle that contracts must comply with all applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: U.S. Constitution, Supremacy Clause, Article VI
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution establishes that the Constitution and federal laws are the supreme law of the land, taking precedence over state laws and even contracts. By allowing the parties to subjectively determine which laws are 'pertinent', the modified clause attempts to circumvent this principle, which is unconstitutional.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text requires adherence to ALL applicable laws. The changed text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity by stating that the agreement is 'generally subject' to laws 'as deemed pertinent by the parties.' This contradicts the principle that contracts must comply with all applicable laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2, derived from Article VI of the U.S. Constitution, establishes the supremacy of federal laws, stating they 'shall be the supreme law of the land...anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding.' The modified contract language, allowing parties to subjectively decide which laws apply, directly undermines this supremacy clause. The change is not a minor softening but an attempt to circumvent mandatory legal compliance, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
